text: Describe a case of left laparoscopic adenectomy in a forty-year-old lady whose hypothyroid came with weak abdominal pain which was generalized in nature.

Her ultrasound was normal, so she was asked for a CT scan in view of her persistent pain, which showed a lesion in the left adrenal which was heterogeneous and cystic in nature, suggestive of an adrenal adenoma.

Her further lab investigations were performed, which showed that her thyroid function tests were normal, her ACTH and basal cortisol levels were normal, and the rest of her investigations were normal, suggestive that this was a non-secretory adrenal tumor.
With this plan in mind, we had planned for laparoscopic left adrenalectomy.
In all our patients with adrenal, we routinely performed a CT scan with adrenal protocol.
The laparoscopic view shows the left adrenal adenoma, clearly demarcated against the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon visualizes the upper pole of the kidney and the normal renal vasculature as they proceed downwards during the laparoscopic left adrenalectomy.
The patient was planned for the surgery.
The patient is positioned in the left lateral position for a laparoscopic left adrenalectomy, utilizing a four-port technique with the monitor placed on the left side to visualize the adrenal gland.
The surgical field shows four ports: two 5mm, one 10mm, and one 12mm, used interchangeably for optical access to provide various views during the adrenalectomy.
The initial optical entry is established, followed by the placement of an additional right five millimeter port for enhanced visualization during the adrenalectomy.
The surgeon utilizes the left five millimeter port and an additional five millimeter port placed in the lower quadrant to aid in mobilizing the spleen and stomach during the procedure.
The surgeon begins mobilizing the splenic flexure using a harmonic scalpel, carefully detaching the tissue to allow better access to the adrenal gland.
The surgeon uses a technique to mobilize the splenic flexure downwards, ensuring the colon is protected from potential injury during the procedure.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel and sharp dissection to mobilize the splenic flexure, causing the colon to gradually fall downwards.
The surgeon continues to mobilize the spleen and pancreas towards the right side, aiming to fully expose the left adrenal gland.
The surgeon continues dissection at the upper pole of the spleen, facilitating its gradual mobilization towards the right side.

The surgeon uses a gauze piece to reflect the spleen, preventing injury during dissection. As the spleen is gradually dissected upwards, the stomach becomes exposed. In the left lower quadrant, the pancreas is visible and being moved aside, allowing exposure of the upper part of the stomach fundus.
This is done completely so that we can prevent any injury to the fundus at the time of manipulation of the gland.
The surgeon exposes the left adrenal gland, revealing the inferior phrenic vein. The vein is followed downward to identify the left adrenal vein, visible in the surgical field.
This is a protocol in all our patients to follow the inferior phrenic vein.
The surgeon performs sharp and blunt dissection to isolate the left adrenal vein, following its course as it becomes visible after previous mobilization of surrounding structures.
The surgeon applies clips to secure the dissected left adrenal vein, ensuring hemostasis following the exposure and dissection of surrounding structures.
The surgeon divides the left adrenal gland after it is fully exposed, as seen in the surgical field.
The surgeon clips and cuts additional blood vessels supplying the left adrenal gland, leaving the inferior phrenic vein as the only intact blood supply visible.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to gradually mobilize the left adrenal gland from its inferior and lateral attachments.
The surgeon completes the separation of the left adrenal gland from the retroperitoneum, ensuring it is fully mobilized and detached from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon separates an additional arterial twig from the left adrenal gland, ensuring no blood loss during the procedure.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to either clip or sacrifice the remaining blood supply to the left adrenal gland, focusing on the inferior phrenic vein.

The surgeon continues the dissection of the adrenal gland in all directions, using a harmonic scalpel to gradually disconnect it from the lower and lateral aspects, visibly separating it from surrounding tissues.
The adrenal gland is visibly suspended by the inferior phrenic vein, which is clipped to complete its detachment.
The surgeon identifies and addresses the final blood supply to the adrenal gland, ensuring complete separation from the surrounding retroperitoneal tissue.
The left adrenal gland is completely freed from surrounding tissues, with all aspects of the gland visibly detached.
The surgeon performs the final dissection and removal of the adrenal gland, completing the adrenalectomy procedure.
The surgeon places the adrenalectomy specimens into an endogenous plastic bag and removes them through a twelve millimeter port using a gallbladder extractor.
The surgical field shows the adrenal bed, which is completely free of tissue and hemostatic, indicating successful completion of the adrenalectomy.
The surgeon carefully repositions the spleen to prevent torsion injuries, ensuring it is securely placed within the abdominal cavity.
The spleen is positioned in its anatomical location, visible within the surgical field.
The surgical specimen is extracted through the twelve millimeter port, completing the removal process.
The surgical field is shown without any drain placement following the completion of the procedure.
In summary, a high BMI lady with a left adrenal adenoma with laparoscopic left adrenalectomy.
The operative time was thirty-six minutes with a blood loss of ten ml with an uneventful post-op recovery.
The surgical specimen is displayed, likely showing the excised adrenal tissue.
And that is the surgical team which helped me through my procedure.
This was performed at the Department of General Surgery, KM Hospital, Mumbai.
Thank you very much.
